comments.body: This post might be helpful: stackoverflow.com/questions/13611108/…

Can you try the below mentioned code and let me know                                TimeZone zone = TimeZone.getTimeZone("America/New_York"); DateFormat format = DateFormat.getDateTimeInstance(); format.setTimeZone(zone);  System.out.println(format.format(new Date()));
So in Turkey on short term notice the DST date was shifted in the time you tested. Normally national authorities are more careful. I would not say that tzdata failed here procedurally.

The legacy date-time API (java.util date-time types and their formatting API, SimpleDateFormat) is outdated and error-prone. The new code should use java.time, the modern date-time API. I highly recommend this great answer by Basil Bourque.

answers.body:
This code is correct. My result New York 1 - Istanbul 9. This problem is your computer or OS. I live in Istanbul and hour of day 9. Summer time application is not over. Because the Turkish elections were postponed for 2 weeks.

    TimeZone zone1 = TimeZone.getTimeZone("America/New_York");
    TimeZone zone2 = TimeZone.getTimeZone("Europe/Istanbul");
    int hour1 = Calendar.getInstance(zone1).get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY);
    int hour2 = Calendar.getInstance(zone2).get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY);

    System.out.println("New York" + hour1 + " - Istanbul "+ hour2);

There seems to be problems with Turkey time as turkey has changed there DST from 1 Nov to 8 Nov

because for the un-updated JVM if i do 

TimeZone timeZone = TimeZone.getTimeZone("Europe/Istanbul");
System.out.println(timeZone.inDaylightTime(new Date()))

It gives me False but as for Istanbul the DST is till NOV 8.

So you need to update the JVM tzdata version from here. Here is what Latest tzdata Data version also says.

tzdata2015g n/a Turkey's 2015 fall-back transition is scheduled for Nov. 8, not Oct. 25. Norfolk moves from +1130 to +1100 on 2015-10-04 at 02:00 local time. Fiji's 2016 fall-back transition is scheduled for January 17, not 24. Fort Nelson, British Columbia will not fall back on 2015-11-01. It has effectively been on MST (-0700) since it advanced its clocks on 2015-03-08. New zone America/Fort_Nelson.

So Refer to this tzupdater documents to get update for your JVM

comments.body: It will be most helpful if the downvoter also shares a critique or comment. (I might have guessed first that the downvote could be because the answer didn’t handle the OP’s real problem, the outdated time zone data; but the answer does indeed handle exactly this issue in a very fine and thorough way.)

answers.body:
tl;dr

Use a JDK vendor tool to install a fresh copy of tzdata.

Use the modern java.time classes.

ZonedDateTime
.of( 
    LocalDate.of( 2025 , Month.SEPTEMBER , 13 ) , 
    LocalTime.of( 1 , 0 ) , 
    ZoneId.of( "America/New_York" )
) 
.withZoneSameInstant(
    ZoneId.of( "Europe/Istanbul" )
)

2025-09-13T08:00+03:00[Europe/Istanbul]

tzdata update

You asked:

tzdata should be automated and the JRE should self update every day.

While I tend to agree with this sentiment, I suppose many sysadmins do not want files being installed surreptitiously without their explicit consent.

So, no automatic updates of tzdata. Some JDK/JRE vendors, such as Oracle and Azul Systems, provide tools for installing updated tzdata.

Every sysadmin & DBA needs to be aware of rule changes to any time zones of interest to their stakeholders. You must then update to a fresh update of Java if it contains your needed tzdata update, or else install a tzdata update yourself.

Java releases arrive every six months, as regular as a train schedule. All JDK products comes bundled with a fresh tzdata. Additionally, some patch releases of JDKs also include fresh tzdata (read their Release Notes). If you make a habit of deploying each of these releases, you will be covered for the time zone rule changes announced well in advance of taking effect. Unfortunately, some politicians are reckless with their zone changes, providing little forewarning. So you may need to update the tzdata manually before a Java release arrives.

Reminder: Besides your JDK/JRE, also update your host OSes, update containerized OSes, and update database engines such as Postgres that bundle their own copy of tzdata

Avoid legacy date-time classes

Avoid the tragically-flawed legacy date-time classes. Never use Calendar, Date, Timestamp, and SimpleDateFormat. Use only the modern java.time classes.

java.time

Right now it is 1 at New York

Use LocalDate to represent a date-only, year-month-day.

LocalDate ld = LocalDate.of( 2025 , Month.SEPTEMBER , 13 ) ;

Use LocalTime to represent a time-of-day.

LocalTime lt = LocalTime.of( 1 , 0 ) ;  // 01:00, 1 AM.

Specify a time zone.

ZoneId zNewYork = ZoneId.of( "America/New_York" ) ;

You should know that EST and such are not real time zones.

Combine to determine a moment, a point on the timeline, by way of ZonedDateTime.

ZonedDateTime zdtNewYork = ZonedDateTime.of( ld , lt , z ) ;

Adjust to another time zone.

ZoneId zIstanbul = ZoneId.of( "Europe/Istanbul" ) ;
ZonedDateTime zdtIstanbul = zdtNewYork.withZoneSameInstant( zIstanbul ) ;

See this code run at Ideone.com. Caveat: That website uses outdated Java 12, so its time zone data is likely outdated. Turkey has been changing its time zone rules in recent years, so beware. See Wikipedia, Time In Turkey. I am no expert on Turkey, but apparently an offset of UTC+03:00 is currently in effect year-round for the country.

Given an offset of -04:00 for America/New_York during Standard Time (not Daylight Saving Time DST), and an offset of +03:00 for Turkey, that totals to Istanbul being 7 hours ahead of New York. Add 7 hours to 1 AM, and we get 8 AM, the result produced by Java 25 (on my own machine).

2025-09-13T01:00-04:00[America/New_York]

2025-09-13T08:00+03:00[Europe/Istanbul]

title: Wrong hour of day in Java calendar
body:
TimeZone zone1 = TimeZone.getTimeZone("America/New_York");
TimeZone zone2 = TimeZone.getTimeZone("Europe/Istanbul");
int hour1 = Calendar.getInstance(zone1).get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY);
int hour2 = Calendar.getInstance(zone2).get(Calendar.HOUR_OF_DAY);

// hour1 gives 1
// hour2 gives 7

Right now it is 1 at New York, but 8 at Istanbul. Why does Java give 1 less result for Europe/Istanbul? Can it be because of daylight savings? If so, doesn't it take care of it automatically?

I am not sure if the problem is because of daylight savings thing. The following shows, I guess, both timezones have DST offset:

zone1.getDSTSavings()/1000L   // gives 3600
zone2.getDSTSavings()/1000L   // gives 3600

zone1.useDaylightTime()   // true
zone2.useDaylightTime()   // true

zone1.inDaylightTime(new Date())   // false
zone2.inDaylightTime(new Date())   // false

SOLVED:

Time zone data was not up to date. Download the tzupdater utility from Java website, and issue:

java -jar tzupdater.jar -l 'http://www.iana.org/time-zones/repository/tzdata-latest.tar.gz'

This auto downloads the latest tz data and updates the JRE. This is a hell. Losing 2 days for a stupid timezone problem. This should be automated and the JRE should self update every day. Why not???

Insert new crontab entry: (Yet another one)

crontab -e
00 05 * * * java -jar tzupdater.jar -l https://www.iana.org/time-zones/repository/tzdata-latest.tar.gz
